segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a white screen displaying the circular 'girafprod' logo. Upbeat, slightly dramatic background music begins as the scene transitions to a bustling urban street, showing various urban pedestrians walking past storefronts, including a prominent ZARA store with mannequins in its window display. Specific individuals captured include a woman with long blonde hair in a white top and red scarf, and a man in a white t-shirt with sunglasses perched on his head, followed by a close-up of a woman with dark hair in a black jacket. A confident female voice states, "We all know Zara. This brand dresses women, men, and children all over the world." This is followed by a rapid succession of enthusiastic voices praising Zara: one female voice exclaims, "Let's just go to Zara, I know I can find something," a male voice adds, "They have very nice stuff that you don't find anywhere else," another female voice with a distinct accent describes Zara as "very special and modern and fashion," while others mention finding new things and "fashion at low cost." The scene shifts to a ZARA storefront at night with a yellow taxi passing by. The female narrator continues, explaining that "In 40 years, it became worldwide with 200 selling points on every continent, and new stores that are sprouting everywhere." An indoor shot then shows a man with long dark hair, wearing a black blazer over a light blue shirt, speaking and gesturing with his hands in front of a wooden wall. A calm, authoritative male voice, possibly with a French accent, discusses Zara's communication strategy, stating, "Zara is one of the rare companies that has a really good communication team in order to not communicate," repeating the latter part in Spanish. The video then shows an exterior night shot of a large illuminated 'INDITEX' sign on a modern building, followed by a view of a glass-fronted modern office building with warm interior lighting. The female narrator identifies Zara as "the crown jewel of Inditex company, the first textile group in the world," describing its headquarters as "a fortress where only a few exterior people have had the privilege to enter." Finally, a man with short gray hair and glasses, wearing a black turtleneck, speaks in front of a bookshelf filled with books, gesturing with his right hand, as the same calm male voice from before shares his impression of the headquarters: "I had the slight impression of being in a James Bond movie when you enter."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a sleek, minimalist fashion showroom featuring mannequins dressed in contemporary clothing, set against abstract pink and black patterns. This transitions to a black screen displaying a blue circular logo with a silhouette of a man and the name 'AMANCIO ORTEGA' in bold orange text. An older woman with curly hair, wearing a checkered blazer and patterned scarf, seated in a blue chair in front of a bookshelf, begins to narrate in a clear, articulate female voice, stating, "Zara is enigmatic, like its founder, Amancio Ortega, who took Bill Gates' title of richest man in the world. Very few people know him." She continues, explaining he is 80 years old, wanted to democratize fashion, and came from a very poor family. The scene shifts to a nighttime arrival of a black car, with a man in a suit exiting amidst a crowd, visually representing the narrator's description of Ortega as "unapproachable." A title card then appears, reading 'ZARA: THE STORY OF THE WORLD'S RICHEST MAN' in bold red and white text, reinforcing the narrator's introduction. The narrator explains, "To tell his story is a challenge because Ortega is unapproachable. But we'll unveil his system, his dazzling success, and also his dark side. We will explore the world according to Zara." Dramatic music swells as an aerial view from an airplane window shows the wing over a landscape. The narrator then guides the viewer, "First, let's go to an austere and remote region where it all began, in the 1960s. Direction Northwest of Spain, in the province of Galicia, away from main roads." A map of Europe zooms into Spain, highlighting A Coruña, followed by a coastal cityscape with modern buildings and a rocky shoreline, and a busy highway with signs for 'Eiris', 'Os Castros', and 'porto', all visually illustrating the journey to the location. The narrator concludes, "A Coruña is a port of 250,000 inhabitants. Amancio Ortega grew up there. Today..." as the segment ends.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a wide shot of a busy highway in Galicia, Spain, featuring road signs for 'ARTEIXO' and 'POLÍGONO', as a female narrator explains that the strategic center of Zara and Inditex headquarters is located in Arteixo, about 10 kilometers from the port, describing it as a 'fortress where one does not enter.' The scene transitions to an aerial view of a modern, glass-fronted office building and a circular structure, likely part of the corporate campus, while the narrator adds that 'in all families in the area, someone has worked for Inditex.' A bustling marina with numerous sailboats and distant cranes under a cloudy sky then appears, representing A Coruña, where the narrator states, 'we do not talk. An Ortega is a mystery, almost a fantasy.' The camera moves to a city street with pedestrians passing 'El café de Macondo', before showing the cozy café interior. A hand then places a book titled 'AMANCIO ORTEGA' on a table, as the narrator introduces Javier Blanco, a journalist and author of Ortega's unauthorized biography, who will describe 'an enigmatic man.' The segment concludes with a seated man, Javier Blanco, with long dark hair, wearing a black blazer, speaking animatedly with a Spanish accent in front of an abstract painting. He explains that Ortega 'prefers anonymity' and 'does not like to appear in the media,' recounting how in 1998, a Portuguese newspaper even 'questioned the existence of Amancio.' Blanco continues, detailing how Inditex had to publish a photo to reassure investors when entering the stock exchange, proving 'there was someone behind the company,' all set to a continuous instrumental background music.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a Spanish voiceover stating, "there was a person who was behind the company," as an upbeat, slightly mysterious background music begins. Visually, a series of international news article headlines about Amancio Ortega from publications like 'LA RAZON' and 'Le Point' are displayed, emphasizing his reclusive nature with phrases such as 'el hombre discreto' and 'the man who didn’t exist'. A female narrator explains that Ortega is "one of the most secret men in the world," who "never granted any interview to the press" and for years "not a single picture of him was available." The headlines feature black-and-white and color images of Ortega, often in formal attire with a serious expression, reinforcing his enigmatic image. The narrator continues, detailing how "without talking about him, without advertising, he amassed a huge fortune, about 80 billion dollars, yet he started from nothing." She describes his story as "like a novel," the tale of "a poor boy, Amancio Ortega, who became the richest man in the world." The visuals then transition to black-and-white archival footage of a coastal city, likely A Coruña, showing a bustling beach, crowded promenades, and military parades, as the narrator explains that "everything starts in A Coruña in the 50s," when his parents settled there "under the regime of General Franco, a dictatorship." The footage shifts to a quiet, narrow street lined with traditional Galician houses, illustrating Ortega's humble beginnings as "the youngest in a family of four," the "son of an employee of the railways," who "grew up" in accommodation for railway workers. The background music continues, underscoring the investigative and reflective tone.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens in a modern, well-lit office space with wooden bookshelves, as light, Spanish-sounding background music begins. An older woman, wearing a checkered blazer and a patterned scarf, walks toward a desk, picks up a book titled 'AMANCIO ORTEGA' by Covadonga O'Shea, and sits down in a distinctive blue ergonomic chair. She opens the book, revealing a portrait of Amancio Ortega on the cover and flipping through pages with photographs and text, including a photo of a woman and a group of people, accompanied by the sound of pages flipping. She then begins speaking directly to the camera in a clear female voice, narrating that Covadonga O'Shea is a legendary fashion journalist in Spain, and identifying the book as being about "my dear Amancio Ortega." She explains that O'Shea had the privilege of recording the only interview he ever gave, from which she drew an authorized biography detailing the incredible journey of this young, disadvantaged man who promised at 13 never to be poor again. Gesturing with her hands, she continues to recount how Amancio Ortega once told her this story over a meal, with his eyes filled with tears, as he recalled why he launched his business. She then shares a personal anecdote, describing a day when her mother picked her up at school, and they went to a grocery store where the owner told her mother, "Josefa, we can't give you credit anymore," a moment that was a "slap" to his mother. As she narrates this, the scene transitions to a dimly lit, warm-toned setting where a man in a dark suit and brown knitted hat is seen from behind, holding a red object—possibly a wallet or phone—while interacting with a woman in a black top and white apron, suggesting a transaction or exchange in a café or bar environment, with soft, golden lighting creating an intimate atmosphere. The background music continues throughout the segment.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a close-up of golden-brown bread baking in a wire rack, glowing warmly under dim lighting, creating a cozy atmosphere. This transitions to an older woman in a blue ergonomic chair, speaking animatedly in front of a bookshelf, holding and flipping through a book titled 'AMANCIO ORTEGA'. Her voice narrates, first in Spanish then translated to English, recounting a moment of "humiliation" that led Amancio to declare, "This will not happen to my mother ever again." A light, melancholic string instrument melody begins to play as the woman continues her narration in English, explaining how this episode was the first trigger propelling the young Amancio into the world of fashion. The scene briefly shifts to a dimly lit café or bar, where a man in a dark suit and brown knitted hat is seen from behind, his eye captured in a moment of intense observation, interacting with a woman in a black top and white apron under warm overhead light. The video then cuts back to the older woman, who continues to narrate that Amancio quit school to earn a living. Archival black-and-white footage then appears, illustrating her words by showing the 'Gala FABRICA DE CAMISAS' clothing store, with its interior displaying neatly arranged shirts, exterior signage, vintage cars parked outside, a neon sign, and a poster of a woman in a dress. The narration explains that Amancio, not yet of legal age, was hired as a handy boy at this 'Gala' shop. The footage continues with a sketchbook displaying fashion illustrations of women in hats and coats, as the narrator details how Amancio left Gala in the early 60s for Maha, a chic store in A Coruña, where he learned the craft of tailoring and had a profound revelation about the world of fashion.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with an older woman, seated in a blue ergonomic chair in front of a wooden bookshelf, wearing a checkered blazer and a patterned scarf. She speaks expressively in Spanish, gesturing with her hands, and at one point holds and flips through a book titled 'AMANCIO ORTEGA'. A narrator translates her words, explaining that Amancio sold items door-to-door by bicycle and an old car, starting in Galicia, specifically A Coruña, by copying robes to sell them cheaper. Light, slightly melancholic instrumental music begins as the scene transitions to grainy, colorized archival footage of a wide city street from the mid-20th century, showing vintage cars under colorful triangular flags and an ornate building with a 'tve1' logo. The narrator continues, detailing Ortega's hard work and how he had no money, borrowing 2,000 pesetas (about 30 euros) from the Bank of Bilbao. The video cuts back to the woman speaking, reinforcing these details. A panoramic view of a modern coastal city under a cloudy sky, featuring a large green field, high-rise buildings, and a water tower, then appears, representing A Coruña as the narrator describes Ortega developing his basic concept and his business becoming a success. This is followed by a moving shot from a vehicle along a city bridge, passing a tall white tower and panning to a waterfront promenade with palm trees and a distant cityscape, while the narrator explains Ortega set up his first workshop, 'Goa', and started hiring. The final scene is an overcast parking lot with a red car, where a woman, identified by the narrator as Sonia Piñeiro, one of Amancio Ortega's first seamstresses, is seen through the window holding a microphone, appearing to be interviewed, with a camera visible outside.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens in a warmly lit domestic room where a woman with short, dark, curly hair, wearing glasses, a blue plaid shirt, and a gold anchor necklace, carefully inspects a white, possibly vintage, dress. The scene transitions to a sepia-toned portrait of a serious-looking man, framed by blurred archival images, as the narrator shifts focus, stating that 'Ortega had flair to surround himself with the best.' segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with upbeat, slightly retro background music as grainy, colorized archival footage of a bustling mid-20th-century city street, featuring vintage cars and colorful triangular flags, transitions to a modern city street with a trolleybus, cars, and a rickshaw under a clear blue sky. A male narrator explains that Ortega decided to diversify and produce all kinds of clothes, opening his first boutique, Zara, in A Coruña in 1975, named after Zadar, Croatia. The scene shifts to a contemporary urban intersection with a blue parking meter and a red-brick building, then cuts to a woman with short, dark, curly hair and glasses, wearing a blue plaid shirt, in a domestic room with wooden furniture, glass cabinets, and framed black-and-white portraits. She carefully inspects a white dress retrieved from a box, examining its 'Confeciones Eva, MADE IN SPAIN' label. A female voice, Sonia, is heard in Spanish, followed by a male translation stating that Zara was initially considered "not very good quality, but it was cheap." The narrator adds that Sonia remembers how they developed the collections. The woman then flips through a fashion magazine, which visually aligns with Sonia's translated recollection of "many magazines from Barcelona" (French and Italian) being used for design inspiration and copying models. The segment concludes with a modern city street featuring a ZARA storefront, tall buildings, and a red 'Fiat' sign, as the narrator explains that the A Coruña store was the first of a long list, and Ortega gradually extended his empire, with a translated male voice adding that "each store opened following the trucks."

The video concludes with a blurred, fast-moving transition before returning to the man, his dialogue progressing.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dreamy, overexposed shot of a hand reaching into a glass water tank, creating ripples, set to upbeat, light background music. The scene transitions to a narrow Parisian street with classic Haussmann-style buildings and a central fountain, as a female narrator introduces the studio of ready-to-wear brand G. Caro. Inside a bright, airy studio, a woman, identified by the narrator as Marguerite Barterot, co-founder of G. Caro, is seen in a colorful, tropical-print shirt, sketching on paper with a marker, surrounded by vibrant artwork and a tropical painting on an easel. The distinct sound of her marker drawing is heard. A man, identified as Philippe Barterot, co-founder, in a beige sweater and scarf, walks through a clothing boutique filled with garments featuring playful, illustrated prints of surfers, birds, and beach scenes. Philippe's voice, speaking in French with a French accent, is heard saying, "I'll leave the skirt here," followed by Marguerite's voice, also in French, responding, "Yes, I'll take a look at it later." The narrator explains that Philippe and Marguerite launched G. Caro five years ago, known for its printed and colorful shirts, which quickly gained attention but also led to imitations. Close-ups show Marguerite's hand drawing colorful, abstract figures, her marker sounds prominent. The camera pans to her face as she speaks passionately in French, expressing her frustration about design theft: "It's really stealing... It's a bit like I was an old lady in the street with my bag and someone grabs it and left... Makes me sad, makes me want to scream." Philippe's voice is heard calmly interjecting "Marguerite." The studio is filled with racks of printed clothing, a laptop, and design materials, emphasizing a creative fashion design workspace. The segment ends with Philippe holding up a white shirt with bird prints, walking through the boutique, as the narrator begins to explain what drives Marguerite.

He then picks up another shirt, this time with a delicate ballerina print, mentioning "Marguerite, the creator of G. Kero, who invented it." The scene widens to show a woman seated at a laptop in the creative studio, filled with racks of unique, illustrated garments, while the man browses. The male voice interjects, noting that Zara already has their copied designs on sale, while their own aren't even released. The female voice concludes, highlighting the challenge for a young brand to compete with a world leader like Zara, which benefits from the "novelty effect." The overall mood is one of creative passion mixed with the frustration of intellectual property challenges in the fashion industry.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Philippe, a man with short brown hair wearing a beige sweater and a patterned scarf, standing in a bright, airy boutique filled with racks of printed children’s clothing. He gestures animatedly as he speaks in French (translated), explaining that they present their collections almost a year in advance, a year before they are sold. He expresses concern about retail chains like Zara sending people to 'sniff out what's going on' and their ability to produce copies in just two months, potentially releasing a copy before the original. As light, slightly melancholic instrumental music begins, a female narrator states that Philippe is concerned about the future of his company, especially because others have followed Zara's plan. Philippe then moves to a table covered in folded garments, picking up a white garment with a bold, multicolored zebra print. He displays it to the camera, identifying it as 'ours' in contrast to a 'Monoprix zebra' and comparing it to designs from J. Crew or Promod. The female narrator adds that this is 'Marguerite and Philippe's version,' emphasizing the need to 'look at the details to see the difference.' Philippe then picks up a light blue dress with a surfer print, displaying it and pointing to specific details on the fabric, such as a small illustrated figure. He elaborates on the graphic composition, mentioning how they 'dress the characters up' and changed colors, pointing out a 'little line' that is 'exactly below' another element, as the camera zooms in on the playful prints of surfers and other motifs, enhancing the cheerful, artistic atmosphere of the creative fashion studio.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a man in a beige sweater and a colorful scarf, speaking passionately in a bright boutique filled with printed garments. His voice, with a French accent, discusses design elements like 'triangles' and 'spacing,' then shifts to express the severe impact on a 'little designer' when their work is copied 'two, three, four, five times,' calling it a 'death sentence.' Light, melancholic background music begins and continues throughout. The scene transitions to a studio workspace where a woman in a tropical-print shirt is sketching. A female narrator's voice takes over, explaining that G.Kero decided to attack Zara for 'counterfeiting and unfair competition,' embarking on a 'long and costly process.' The visual then shifts to a screen displaying an email in Spanish, addressed to Inditex, with an English translation overlay. The narrator reads from this email, stating, 'We noticed that several brands accused you of copying some of their models. We found surprising similarities between models of the French brand G.Kero and Zara's. We would like to have your opinion on it.' The narrator continues, revealing that 'Zara did not respond to our email' to date, and discusses the broader implications in the fashion industry where copying a competitor risks being sued and facing a 'huge fine,' though proving it is 'much more' difficult. This is shown over an exterior shot of a ZARA storefront and a close-up of a floral dress on a mannequin, visually linking the accusations to the brand's aesthetic.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a floral dress on a mannequin, displaying vibrant pink, red, yellow, and blue flowers on a cream background, accompanied by the 'sandro' brand name. This transitions to a full-body shot of a woman in a black leather biker jacket over a navy top and white pants, again with the 'sandro' logo visible. A female narrator explains that "Sandra Brand has filed a complaint against Zara," pointing out "many similarities" in "the top of the leather jacket, biker style," and "the snaps at the end." The scene then shows a split-screen comparison of two black leather biker jackets from Sandro and ZARA, highlighting their similar asymmetrical zippers, silver hardware, and shoulder epaulets, as the narrator reveals "The French justice condemns Zara to pay 200,000 euros to Sandro." An exterior shot of a ZARA storefront with large glass windows and mannequins in dark outfits, along with a metallic ZARA sign, follows. The narrator then states, "But don't think that it's always like that," introducing the idea that "the brands that have lost against Zara are numerous," including "some very prestigious" ones. The video cuts to a grid of high-heeled shoes, including stilettos, platform pumps, and peep-toes in bold colors and textures, with a close-up on black suede platform heels featuring signature red soles. The narrator identifies these as creations of "famous French shoe designer Christian Louboutin," whose "creations across the world for their famous red sole, found on all models," are recognized, and which he "copyrighted." The segment concludes with a side-by-side comparison of a red patent leather peep-toe slingback and a similar ZARA version with an espadrille-style platform, as the narrator prompts, "But look at this pair of Zara shoes, inspired by this model from Louboutin," emphasizing the design similarities between luxury and fast-fashion footwear. Throughout, an upbeat, slightly dramatic background music plays.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a woman with gray hair tied back, wearing a black top and a white scarf, speaking expressively in a bright, airy home office. She gestures with a hand adorned with a distinctive silver ring, explaining in a clear voice how fashion experts understand that trends from the 70s and 80s are returning. She articulates that when Zara is accused of imitation, they can counter by saying, "Yes, I put rabbits on a shirt, but look, this shirt from the 70s also has rabbits," implying cyclical design. As she speaks, the scene cuts to a display of colorful tropical-print shirts featuring parrots, palm trees, and surfers, hanging on wooden hangers. The video returns to the woman, her long dangling earrings catching the light, as she continues her commentary, emphasizing that "imitating and repeating is the base of the fashion system." A close-up reveals her hand resting on a printed photograph, underscoring her engagement with visual references. The scene then shifts to a computer screen displaying a French news article titled 'Zara ou l'histoire du prêt à porter cheap qui copie le luxe,' with visible Facebook elements, directly illustrating her discussion of Zara’s design practices. The woman reappears, speaking with conviction about how "fashion does by remaking" and that "copying is difficult to prove, so Zara continues to draw inspiration from the creations of others." Throughout, a light background music plays. The video then shows a flat lay of various garments, including floral blouses, a plaid shirt, and a green patterned top, followed by a mannequin wearing a black jacket with a geometric pattern and a vibrant floral dress with multicolored blooms, culminating in a close-up of the intricate floral print on the dress, showcasing the Zara aesthetic she describes as what customers "come picking in store."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a bustling urban street scene, showcasing a modern ZARA storefront with large glass windows, as an upbeat background music track begins. A female voiceover immediately states that Zara captures all the big brand trends, but cheaper, bringing them quickly to the store. The camera then focuses on a woman in a black coat with a fur-lined hood, speaking directly to the camera in the lively city environment, likely one of the interviewees heard stating in French, "they already have it, they already have it, they already have it," emphasizing Zara's speed. The voiceover continues, explaining that Zara copies designs, with another interviewee adding in French, "It's an obvious thing," and others noting, "Sometimes they copy a little bit," specifically mentioning shirts. The scene transitions to a series of split-screen comparisons, visually highlighting design similarities between luxury items and Zara versions. These include a black studded Zara ankle boot juxtaposed with a red Chloé boot, and a black and white floral Zara dress mirroring an Isabel Marant 2013 design, while the voiceover explicitly mentions having a "crush on this little Isabel Marant top, those Chloé boots, or this Dior dress," and finding "almost the same models at Zara, but much cheaper." Interviews with other women on the street are interspersed, including two women in front of a ZARA HOME store—one in a black polka-dot top and another in a striped shirt—and another pair near the ZARA storefront—one in a blue blazer and sunglasses, the other in a floral top—all engaging in conversation. The voiceover reveals that attempts to contact the press services of the luxury brands were met with refusal, as "it is simply bad publicity" for them. The segment concludes with a young woman in a black hoodie with a white graphic standing in front of the ZARA entrance, followed by a final split-screen comparison of a Celine graphic dress and its ZARA counterpart, reinforcing the theme of fast fashion imitating high fashion, as the voiceover trails off.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a street-level view of a ZARA storefront, its large glass windows displaying mannequins in dark, stylish outfits, including a floral dress and a black jacket with floral embroidery. As the camera pans across the display, capturing reflections of the street and buildings, a female narrator explains that Zara understood how showcases give the brand its image, integrating this strategy from the start, and that these showcases are designed in great secret in the bunker of A Coruña. The scene then shifts to a modern glass building with a green lawn in front, possibly the Inditex premises mentioned by the narrator. This transitions to an indoor setting where Mark Tungate, a man with gray hair and glasses, wearing a black turtleneck, is seated at a desk with a laptop and his book titled 'FASHION BRANDS'. He types, then turns to speak directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, describing his experience entering Inditex. With a French accent, he likens it to arriving at a villain's lair in a "James Bond movie," detailing a lift, a lobby where "everything was white, white, white, very minimalist," and "a whole street of showcases which are called pilot stores," comparing the atmosphere to a "ghost town." The background features a bookshelf, creating a scholarly atmosphere. The video then cuts to a 3D-rendered fashion showroom, visually echoing Tungate's description, with white mannequins in black outfits standing in a minimalist space with pink abstract light patterns on the walls, suggesting a digital or conceptual design environment.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a sleek, minimalist 3D-rendered fashion showroom, showcasing white mannequins in black and white outfits against a clean white space with pink abstract light patterns, suggesting a digital design environment. As the scene transitions to a dimly lit workshop, a woman in a colorful tropical-print shirt sketches while a man in a beige sweater and scarf examines printed garments featuring surfers and birds, gesturing toward them in a collaborative studio setting. A male voice-over, possibly with a French accent, explains that "they create with their team the image that will be the image of the season and that will be transmitted to all the shops around the world." The narrative then shifts to real-world ZARA storefronts on bustling city streets, with pedestrians and a yellow taxi passing by, followed by a close-up of a mannequin inside. A female voice-over, with an American accent, describes these as "ghost stores" that are "unique in the fashion world," emphasizing that "the brand cares about getting the best locations," reportedly paying "300 million euros for the store located on Fifth Avenue in New York." She continues, stating that "Zara invests heavily in its stores" and that "those displays you immediately notice are located in the nicest neighborhoods," serving as "Zara's best publicity." Exterior shots of ZARA locations, including one on Rue de Rivoli, are shown, leading to a wide view of the Arc de Triomphe in Paris with traffic flowing through the Champs-Élysées. The final scenes show a blurred, warm-lit café with outdoor seating, where a woman in a black coat enters, creating a cozy urban evening atmosphere, as the female narrator explains, "To understand the Zara system, we take an appointment in Paris with a man working for a competing company," who has "studied Zara's operations" for months and finds "the brand's inner workings are frightening." Throughout the segment, a subtle, investigative background music track plays.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dimly lit, cozy café featuring outdoor seating with white tablecloths, greenery, candles, and a 'Paparazzi' sign visible through the glass. A female voice begins, stating, "He agreed to meet in a discreet place, anonymously." The scene then cuts to a dark silhouette of a man in profile, backlit by warm interior lights, before shifting to a bustling Parisian street with traffic and a newspaper kiosk displaying a 'LIU JO' advertisement. A male voice, speaking with a slight accent, explains, "I tried to crack the model to understand how this model works and what I could learn for my own business," continuing in French, "pour ma propre entreprise," before elaborating on financial models and advertising budgets, noting, "there are no expenses in advertising displays or television." The video transitions to a television screen showing a man in a white sweatshirt receiving a newspaper and later smiling as he walks outside a building. The female voice returns, analyzing, "Such positioning is unique in the fashion world," and discussing the highly competitive textile market, citing H&M and Zara as examples, where H&M "calls in singers or football players to create a buzz." Upbeat, jazzy background music briefly plays. The female voice continues, explaining that other retailers spend significantly on advertising, but "Not Zara." A different female voice then speaks rapidly in French, "Zara c'est pas une marque qui est portée sur les red carpets," which the first female voice translates, "Zara is not a brand that is worn on red carpets. So they went for a low profile." The French voice adds, "C'est rare moi. They built themselves on other..." as the segment concludes with a woman with gray hair, wearing a black top and white scarf, speaking directly to the camera in a bright, airy room with white columns.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a woman in a black top and white scarf, speaking directly to the camera in a bright, airy room, explaining that brands advertise because they are not inherently desired, unlike a 'coveted brand' like Zara. As she speaks, the video transitions to close-ups of fashion products, including a cream-colored knitted sweater with ribbed detailing, vibrant orange high-heeled sandals with a buckle strap, and a black strappy high-heeled sandal with cut-out details, while an upbeat, light background music starts. The commentary shifts to Zara, describing it as 'a different brand' and highlighting its visionary founder, Ortega, who 'revolutionized fashion' by inventing 'fast fashion,' producing and delivering in 'record time' without traditional seasonal collections. Visually, an exterior shot of a modern ZARA storefront on a bustling city street shows urban pedestrians, including a woman in a black coat with a fur-lined hood and a man in a white shirt, walking past. The speaker notes Zara's ability to stock a store just '15 days after it has been drawn,' and that items are 'permanently out of stock' as part of their system. A woman is then seen exiting the ZARA store with a black shopping bag, followed by another woman in a light blue top adjusting her collar while holding a black-and-white striped bag. The scene then shifts to a dimly lit café interior, where a dark silhouette of a man is seen in profile, backlit by warm interior lights, creating a mysterious atmosphere. The camera slowly pans to reveal more silhouettes of people seated at tables, with a chandelier hanging above and arched windows showing the street outside. A male voiceover concludes the segment, explaining that unlike normal textile stores where being out of stock is problematic, 'not at Zara,' because 'out of stock is part of their marketing investment.'

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a mysterious silhouette of a man in profile, backlit by warm interior lights in a dimly lit café, creating a contemplative atmosphere. This visual is interspersed with a brightly lit clothing store displaying neutral-toned garments, where a woman browses, and later returns to the café, revealing a chandelier and arched windows, emphasizing the contrast between dark interiors and illuminated backgrounds. Over these scenes, a male voice discusses the frustration that drives customers back to stores, noting the atypical speed at which products become available. A female narrator then highlights the brand's rapid marketing of new models, a point further elaborated by another male voice explaining that while the fashion industry typically takes 40 weeks from design to market, Zara achieves this in just two to four weeks, making their process "extremely quickly." The scene then shifts to a bustling Parisian street with pedestrians crossing in front of a ZARA storefront, a visual backdrop to the ongoing discussion about Zara's efficiency and customer loyalty, with the female narrator noting that Zara customers visit stores 17 times a year compared to competitors' 3-4 times. Finally, the camera focuses on individual women: a woman in a gray beanie and green coat states in French (translated) that she visits Zara "three to four times a week," followed by a woman in a camouflage jacket and sunglasses confirming in English that she shops at Zara "about three times a week," and a woman with curly blonde hair gesturing as she mentions "sometimes in the United States..." All these spoken testimonials reinforce the brand's high customer engagement.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens on a lively city street in front of a ZARA HOME store, where two women, one in a black polka-dot top and the other in a black-and-white striped shirt, smile and speak to the camera, with their conversation including phrases like "Each week" and "Oh yeah, yeah, no" punctuated by laughter. The scene quickly transitions to a bustling urban environment, showing pedestrians, including a woman in a black coat with a fur-lined hood and a man in a white shirt, walking past a ZARA storefront. A female narrator's voice explains that Zara invented "ultra-fast supply, fast fashion," a model now adopted by many other brands. The camera then shifts to a wide shot of the Arc de Triomphe in Paris, with modern buildings and a large 'ESSEC' sign visible, as the narrator highlights that Zara's unique winning strategy is taught in most business schools, "like here in Paris." The scene moves indoors to a lecture hall at ESSEC Business School, where Jérôme Barthelemy, a man in a dark blazer and light blue shirt, identified by the narrator as "a professor and specialist in corporate strategy," is giving a presentation to a group of students. The projector screen displays French questions about Zara’s business strategy, such as 'Amancio Ortega est-il un visionnaire?' and 'Comment Zara parvient-elle à être rentable alors qu'elle produit ses vêtements en petites quantités et en Europe?'. Jérôme gestures expressively as he speaks in French, with an English voice-over explaining how most companies are "doomed to mediocrity" and Zara's originality lies in taking "received ideas and common practices" to follow trends, adapt, and get products looking alike in less than 15 days. Natural light streams in from large windows behind him, revealing an atrium with people walking above, emphasizing the global reach and academic analysis of Zara’s business model.

This man further elaborates on Ortega's social life, mentioning his connections to the daughter of Infanta Elena and King Juan Carlos of Spain, and his interactions with government heads, linking these to the company's growth. The scene then shifts to a wide aerial view of a coastal cityscape under a cloudy sky, featuring a curved road, a water tower, and a modern circular building, suggesting A Coruña, Spain. The female narrator returns to discuss 'the future King of...' before a blurred transition ends the segment, accompanied by light, investigative background music throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a cluttered metal shelving unit, featuring stacks of papers and files, and a framed 'FAST FASHION' poster, as a female narrator explains that 'Spain is not welcomed in A Coruña as he should be.' The scene transitions to a woman with short, dark, curly hair, wearing glasses and a black turtleneck, typing intently on a silver MacBook in a bright home office, while the narrator continues, 'In our case, we have no chance of getting into Zara.' A close-up follows of a hand in a plaid shirt writing in a notebook, then another close-up of hands typing on a laptop keyboard, as the narrator adds, 'Yet, it's not for lack of trying. Despite dozens of calls and emails.' The focus shifts to a screen displaying a forwarded email in French from INDITEX, dated October 27, 2015, declining a visit due to 'la complexité de l'emploi du temps de la société,' with the narrator confirming, 'Our requests for interviews with officials, and our authorizations to shoot in shops or factories of the Spanish group were unsuccessful.' Upbeat, slightly tense background music begins. The scene cuts to a woman in a red-and-white plaid shirt, wearing glasses, talking on a landline phone at a cluttered desk, with a framed photo of Amancio Ortega and ZARA sales figures prominently displayed. The camera slowly zooms in on her focused expression as a phone rings, then clicks. The woman, speaking with a slight accent, asks, 'I would like to talk to someone of the communication, please,' to which a different female voice replies, 'Okay, I'll be through in one second, please.' The woman thanks her, and the phone rings again. The narrator comments, 'The communication service is reachable. The reception desk friendly, but then no answer. Zara is mute,' as the phone continues to ring. The final shot is of a man with long dark hair, wearing a black blazer, speaking animatedly in front of a large abstract painting, his voice heard stating, 'Zara is one of the few companies that has a very good communication team for not communicating,' as the background music continues.

The female voiceover returns, explaining these tracks send "5 million clothes to Zara stores every week worldwide," with the goal "always to gain more time." The scene shifts to a man in a dark blazer and light blue shirt, speaking in a bright, airy room with large windows, though his voice is not heard directly. The video then returns to the automated warehouse, showing close-ups of labeled packages and automated sorting. The segment concludes with the man with long dark hair continuing his interview, as the English male voiceover reinforces that "Creation and production, everything's done internally in order to go as fast as possible."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a mannequin's lower body, showcasing a geometric-patterned skirt and a black handbag. The scene quickly transitions to a man with long dark hair, wearing a black blazer, speaking expressively in front of a large abstract painting. He is heard speaking in Spanish, followed by an English female voice stating, "For that, Zara employs an army of designers." A Spanish male voice then adds, "Hay muchísimos diseñadores," which is echoed by an English male voice confirming, "There are hundreds of designers around the world." The man continues to gesture as the English male voice describes designers traveling with "a camera," quickly corrected by a Spanish male voice to "con una sencilla libreta y un lápiz" (with a simple notebook and a pencil) to observe fashion. The English male voice then details how they "watch how the kids dress up in Tokyo" and "what they wear in New York," while a Spanish male voice adds "what's happening in Paris" and "what's happening in Milan," and both voices discuss "how they breathe in Singapore." The setting then shifts to a wide shot of a modern fashion studio or showroom, where several people are engaged in design work, followed by a close-up of a hand drawing a detailed fashion sketch. The scene changes again to a dimly lit, cluttered workshop where three people are working on garments, including a woman operating a vintage black Singer sewing machine and another cutting fabric with blue-handled scissors. During this, an English female voice explains, "In A Coruña, 250 designers work on the collections. In total, more than 18,000 different models are sold each year." The final segment features an older woman with curly hair, wearing a checkered blazer and a patterned scarf, seated in a blue ergonomic chair in front of a bookshelf. The English female voice continues, "Hard to believe that for Ortega, it all started in the dining room of his brother." The woman then speaks, alternating between English and Spanish, describing Ortega as "a very simple person, intuitive with brilliant ideas," who "had really clear ideas" and "wanted to democratize fashion," emphasizing that "he came from a very poor family" as she gestures and occasionally flips through a book titled 'AMANCIO ORTEGA' that she holds in her lap.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with an older woman, seated in a blue ergonomic chair in front of a bookshelf, speaking expressively in Spanish. Wearing a checkered blazer and a patterned scarf, she gestures while stating, translated as, "He doesn't want to be seen. A part of him is so simple and real with humility... He does not want to show off... I haven't done all this alone. A lot of people have done it with me. And it's not fair that people think that I did it alone... that they think that I am the one who did it." As light, slightly mysterious background music begins, the scene transitions to a wide coastal cityscape under a cloudy sky, showing modern buildings along a waterfront. A narrator explains that this "strategy of discretion and simplicity is respected by Ortega's bodyguards," and that "Inditex executives live in this soulless neighborhood in Laros," which is called "Zarapolis." The camera pans to a modern beige building, identified by an 'URBANIZACION ICARIA' sign, secured by private security, where a red car is parked and the number '13' is visible on a black door, illustrating the "uniform and airtight houses, all under surveillance." The view shifts to a rocky shoreline with people walking, then to 'EL RELUJO RESTAURANTE' with a red awning and outdoor patrons, which the narrator identifies as the "quite simple restaurant" where "Zaristocrates" – described as multi-millionaires – go, being "as low profile as their boss." After a blurry coastal view, the video cuts to a bustling city street with tall buildings, including a ZARA storefront where pedestrians, like a woman in a black coat and a man in a white shirt, walk past. The narrator concludes, "From here, you would not believe it, but those Zaristocrates are masters of the world," as the music becomes more dramatic, hinting at "Their empire" while showing a modern city street with a red 'Fiat' sign and a dome-topped structure.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a bustling urban scene featuring a ZARA storefront, where pedestrians, including a woman in a black coat with a fur-lined hood, walk past under bright daylight, and a yellow taxi drives by. A female narrator introduces ZARA's global reach, stating that billions of people travel the world in comfortable, cheap, and interchangeable clothes made on all continents, far from A Coruña. She continues, noting that 40 years after opening its first store, the world belongs to Zara, as upbeat, slightly mysterious background music begins. The scene transitions to a man with long dark hair, wearing a black blazer, speaking expressively in front of a large abstract painting with black and red splatters. The narrator explains that until the 2000s, ZARA's clothes were made in Galicia and Spain, but growth necessitated globalized production. This is visually reinforced by a world map highlighting Asia (35%), Spain, Portugal, and Morocco (49%), and Latin America (2%), as the narrator specifies that 35% are manufactured in Asia against 49% in the Spain area. The music continues, accompanied by the sound of a passing vehicle, as a man with shoulder-length dark hair walks down a narrow European street before entering a cozy café. Inside the warmly lit café, with wooden furniture and a forest painting, he sits with two women, discussing documents, a laptop, and a newspaper with fashion illustrations. The narrator identifies this man as Juan, the head of an association of workers in St. John the Compostela, Galicia, who contests ZARA's outsourcing figures, attributing high unemployment in Spain partly to the massive outsourcing of textiles. segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens in a dimly lit café with warm yellow walls, where Juan, a man with shoulder-length dark hair wearing a denim jacket over a gray shirt, sits with two companions. He holds a newspaper titled 'LA TRASTENDA DE INDITEX' and gestures while speaking in Spanish, discussing the employment crisis in Galicia, noting over 20% unemployment. A laptop on the table displays a Spanish news article with the headline 'En algunos países si quitas a los chicos de trabajar es peor' and a video playing a fashion event with the text 'ABRIMOS LOS OJOS' and 'SANTIAGO DE COMPOSTELA'. A female narrator explains, over background music, that textile giants like Inditex increase profits by producing where labor is cheaper, while their home countries face crisis and need jobs. Juan continues, clarifying that their group fights against slavery, not specifically Zara, but against many multinational companies across various sectors that seek the cheapest possible production, citing Zara as an example. The scene then shifts to a dynamic urban environment under natural daylight, as Juan, now in a brown jacket and carrying a red-and-black backpack, walks down a city street past urban buildings, a 'tackia' store, and a 'BANCA' sign. He crosses a pedestrian crossing, turns to the camera with a smile, as the female narrator states he is bringing them to a Zara store to support his statements. Juan then suggests they enter the shop to check labels and their origin.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens inside a ZARA store, where a man with shoulder-length dark hair, wearing a brown jacket over a blue shirt, actively browses clothing racks. Amidst background music and indistinct chatter, a male narrator immediately challenges the myth that most ZARA production is Spanish, stating, "I bet you won't find here any labels that say made in Spain." The man meticulously examines garments, picking up a dark floral-patterned shirt and a cream-colored knitted sweater, revealing a 'ZARA MAN' tag and 'MADE IN CHINA' details. He then inspects a dark coat with a fur-lined collar, noting a red tag indicating 'MADE IN PAKISTAN'. The narrator continues to list manufacturing countries like "Turkey," "Morocco," and "China," emphasizing the absence of "Spain" labels. He specifically highlights Pakistan as a country "where it's well known that workers have no rights," explaining, "That's why they manufacture there." As the man continues to check other garments, the narrator suggests, "Let's get out before they see us," coinciding with the man walking through the store and exiting through glass doors onto a bustling city street. Outside, he passes the ZARA storefront, urban pedestrians, and other businesses like a 'BANCA' sign and a 'tackia' store. He turns to smile at the camera, carrying a red-and-black backpack, as a female narrator concludes, "In the third world, textile giants do not always control their supply."

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a somber, slightly tense instrumental background music as a narrator details how "scandals multiplied" in recent years. Visually, a distressing scene unfolds with a green Caterpillar bulldozer amidst a rubble pile, surrounded by rescue workers, illustrating the 2005 collapse of the Spectrum factory, a Zara subcontractor in Bangladesh, which killed 64 people. The narrator explains these workers toiled seven days a week for just 10 euros per month, leading Inditex to set up a compensation fund. Four years later, the focus shifts to 2011, as the video shows a news article from MailOnline headlined 'Zara accused of employing children as young as 14 in 'slave labour' factories in Brazil'. The narrator confirms Zara is "still getting talked about," this time in South America, where suppliers employed workers in "slave-like conditions." This is followed by a ZARA storefront at night, where protesters hold signs, visually representing the public scrutiny. A close-up of a ZARA label on black clothing reinforces the brand's involvement. The narrator adds that in Argentina, the Alameda Foundation filmed raids in "sordid sweatshops," visually suggested by a dimly lit, cluttered room with a bed, TV, and a pile of black garments. Inditex acknowledged these irregularities, stating suppliers used these methods without informing them. The segment concludes with a computer screen displaying the INDITEX website's 'Code for manufacturers and suppliers' page, outlining ethical standards, as the narrator explains Inditex strengthened this code "to clean up its image."

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a close-up of a computer screen displaying the INDITEX 'Code for manufacturers and suppliers' page, where a female narrator reads aloud ethical standards such as 'No forced labour', 'No child labour', 'No harsh or inhumane treatment', 'Reasonable working hours', 'The right to remuneration', and 'Environmental pledge', noting these were established in 2012. As rhythmic, investigative background music begins, the scene transitions to a digital map of North Africa, zooming into Tunisia, specifically pinpointing Tunis and Monastir. The narrator explains they went to Tunisia because wages are lower than in China and Inditex has many subcontractors there, with the main textile production center near Monastir, 200 kilometers from Tunis, where 450 garment enterprises are gathered. Visuals then show a coastal town with white buildings, a prickly pear cactus with red fruit, and three men carrying large white rolls of material along a roadside, passing a road sign pointing to 'Mahdia' and 'Monastir'. The narrator continues, stating they will see how clothing is produced for Zara, introducing Wasim Zalia, a former Levi's employee who invested 1500 euros to set up a textile factory and goes to Mahdia daily. The scene shifts to the interior of a moving car, showing Wasim Zalia in a green jacket driving, looking upward as the car travels along a tree-lined road. The final shot shows a black Mercedes-Benz with license plate '183-101-06' driving on a paved road, approaching a crosswalk, as Wasim Zalia speaks with an accent, stating, "On today, thank you God, I have 350 employees and I work with the Inditex group."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with an upbeat, slightly exotic instrumental tune as a black Mercedes-Benz sedan, license plate '183-101-06', drives along a sunlit road towards the 'SIP PLUS Confection' garment manufacturing facility, identified by its Arabic and French sign as a customs-supervised factory. A female narrator reveals this is "Wassim's company, Sip Confection," which "mainly manufactures clothes for Zara." The man in a green jacket, Wassim, exits the car and enters the building, where he is greeted by a receptionist in a red hijab. Inside, the constant hum of sewing machines and general factory sounds fill the air. Wassim exchanges greetings in Arabic, saying "Sabah. Labas." and later "Ah, nedi, sba khir," as he proceeds into the large, brightly lit factory floor. The narrator explains that "here, as in the entire textile industry in Tunisia, 85% of employees are women," who are "between 16 and 35 years old, and work 48 hours a week" in accordance with Tunisian law, which Wassim respects. The factory is bustling with these women, in light blue uniforms and colorful hijabs, operating Brother sewing machines and assembling garments. The narrator specifies that "all the employees are working on a very big order of shirts for Zara," and Wassim and his foremen "must quickly deliver 10,000 pieces." Wassim walks through the production area, observing the workflow, and interacts with a supervisor in a white lab coat bearing the 'SIP PLUS Confection' logo. He asks for a shirt in Arabic ("A'tini shi qamis"), to which the supervisor responds "malfa." The supervisor then shows him racks of finished white shirts, individually wrapped in plastic. Wassim examines a garment closely, touching the fabric and checking the stitching, confirming in English, "This is a Zara shirt. It's a woman." The clean, well-organized environment, with fluorescent lighting and yellow storage crates, underscores the scale and direct oversight of the manufacturing process.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a white garment, plastic-wrapped and tagged, hinting at a production environment. The scene quickly transitions to a bustling garment factory, filled with the constant whirring and clatter of industrial sewing machines, creating a methodical background hum. Multiple women, wearing colorful hijabs and light blue uniforms, are diligently seated at Brother sewing machines, guiding fabric with precision and speed. A male voice, speaking in French, mentions 'for export' as a man in a green jacket, identified by a female narrator as 'Wassim,' walks through the factory. Wassim, whose role is to 'check everything' to ensure no poorly finished products are delivered, observes the workers and interacts with a supervisor in a white coat and black hijab. The supervisor guides him, showing stacks of finished white garments. A male voice explains that one worker is responsible for 'launching, labeling, each piece,' and that 'ironing' is also done here. The female narrator emphasizes the importance of this order for Wassim and the factory's future. The brightly lit factory features stacked yellow and green plastic crates, indicating an organized space. A male voice details the impressive production rate: 'This room makes 1200 pieces per day and 150 pieces per hour.' One worker, wearing a black hijab, is seen using a smartphone displaying a chronometer, which the female narrator explains is part of a system where 'each worker is timed,' called 'working to the minute.' A male voice specifies the target: 'For the shirt they have 38 minutes, 38 minutes for the finished shirt.' The narrator then introduces 'Jamila,' a worker responsible for production calculations, who 'does not take her eyes off her watch,' as exceeding the 38-minute target would cause the company to 'lose money,' making workers subject to regular checks. The overall atmosphere is industrious and highly efficient, driven by strict timing and quality control.

The scene transitions to a wide shot of a busy garment factory floor, where multiple women in light blue uniforms and colorful hijabs operate white Brother sewing machines, their rhythmic motions visible as the camera pans. The narrator highlights that thanks to these low production costs, Wasim makes a profit, but Zara is the true winner, as a shirt sold for €29 (three times its production cost) is shown in clear plastic packaging with a '€29.99' price tag. The scene shifts to an exterior shot of a factory building, then pans along a road to a 'Denim Services Tunisie' sign. segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens inside a large, brightly lit industrial garment factory, characterized by high ceilings and exposed ductwork. A woman in a green jacket, acting as a visitor or inspector, walks through the organized workstations. A voiceover explains that this is where clothes are dyed and jeans faded. She approaches a large, circular industrial washing machine, which is identified by the speaker as 'the machine where we take care of the Zara shirts,' ready to be dyed in ecru. Transitioning into a laboratory or quality control area, she observes a supervisor in a white coat and black hijab, who is handling large glass flasks containing dark liquids. The supervisor's voice explains that these are 'the dyes,' specifically 'direct' dyes, pointing out 'pink' and 'beige' examples used for 'the Zara shirt.' The visitor then examines finished garments, including a white shirt with a €29.99 price tag, as the supervisor states, 'And here's the final result,' adding that the 'cost of the operation' is '2 euros,' charged by 'Demnem Services to Wassim.' The scene shifts to a denim production area where factory workers in light blue uniforms operate Brother sewing machines. A worker is seen using a spray gun to apply a finish to a pair of jeans. The supervisor's voice continues, detailing that 'many toxic products are used' to achieve the desired color, including 'chemical agents, fixators, acetic acid,' which are 'very corrosive to the skin, mucous membranes, or the lungs.' Safety signage for 'ACIDE ACETIQUE' and 'AGENT DE CATIONISATION' is visible on the walls, reinforcing the supervisor's warning that these chemicals are 'dangerous' and can 'easily attack the stomach,' being the source of 'the majority of diseases, cancers.' Despite these dangers, the supervisor notes, 'those products are handled with bare hands.' The segment concludes with a close-up of a denim garment undergoing chemical treatment, emphasizing the precision and industrial scale of the operation.

Behind them, a pair of jeans hangs from a white industrial machine labeled 'OXY' with Arabic text 'مصنع' (factory). A female narrator sets a critical tone, stating that management seems unconcerned about worker protection. The scene shifts to a close-up of a large circular machine, then to a worker in a white coat inspecting and folding denim garments. The camera then focuses on a worker in a teal shirt applying a chemical finish to jeans with a spray gun, showcasing the denim’s texture and stitching. During this, the woman in the lab coat's voice is heard asking the worker, "Why do you not wear gloves?" The worker's male voice responds, stating it's "not practical" because he wants to "work fast," even as the woman warns that it's "dangerous for your skin" and the product is "very dangerous for your hand." The narrator explains that performance is vital, and workers risk losing their jobs if they don't work fast enough, leading them to prioritize speed over health. The worker in the teal shirt is then seen pouring liquid from a white container into a large blue barrel with a hazard symbol, amidst an industrial environment with visible pipes, metal structures, and chemical storage, suggesting a garment finishing or dyeing facility. The narrator concludes by questioning the toxicity of the chemicals used for dyeing.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a wide shot of a coastal cityscape, showcasing white and beige buildings along a shoreline with dry reeds in the foreground under a clear blue sky, accompanied by somber, investigative background music. A female voiceover explains that textile factories, which are catastrophic for the environment, are predominantly located along the Bay of Monastir. The scene transitions to Mounir Hassine, President of the Tunisian Forum for Economic and Social Rights, a man in a dark brown coat and glasses, stepping out of a dusty silver car parked on an arid, sunlit roadside near a busy highway. As he walks along a dirt path beside a weathered concrete curb, gesturing toward the surrounding area, his voice becomes prominent over the sounds of passing traffic. He points to a concrete drainage ditch, filled with murky water and floating debris like plastic bags, exclaiming, "Look at this. This is the water that washed jeans." He continues, explaining that this waste is "poured directly here" and "arrives from the factories there," specifying that "three there" are dumping into this "canal." As trucks, a yellow taxi, and a white van pass on the highway, Mounir Hassine gestures toward the traffic and the polluted ditch, highlighting "the contributions made by those factories that are dumping here directly." He emphasizes that this untreated waste "goes directly into the bay, haphazardly, just like that, you see?" The sequence concludes with him standing near the highway, still gesturing, as he directs attention to "the coast there" and "all those plants," underscoring the ecological disaster.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a man in a dark brown coat and glasses, speaking with a distinct French accent beside a busy highway, gesturing toward a polluted drainage ditch. He states that "before they were not there. It was safe. And this bay was an asset." The camera pans to reveal a wide, arid wetland area with patches of dry reeds and stagnant, murky water, showing signs of environmental degradation, including close-up shots of a yellow plastic bottle and a black plastic bag submerged in the ditch. A female narrator explains that "Monastir's Bay was renamed the Triangle of Death," formerly known for its pure waters but now a "dumping ground where untreated waste and chemicals are discharged." The man continues speaking, now positioned near a white truck and a line of buses with highway traffic behind him, noting that "the authorities are aware of this" pollution but "they don't do anything about it because they connect the pollution problem with the employment problem." He elaborates on the dilemma, asking "what can we do? If we press those factories there, there'll be more unemployment." The narrator adds that "the whole world wants to buy cheap clothes, and it has a cost," and "environmental protection is not the government's priority." The scene then shifts to a bright, sunlit factory courtyard where several women in light blue uniforms and colorful hijabs, some with children, are seated on the ground. A supervisor in a white coat with a 'SIP PLUS Confection' logo walks through the industrial setting, as the narrator concludes by mentioning that "Amon Tiga employs 150,000 people around the globe," implying a connection to the factory and the earlier discussion of employment versus environmental protection. Throughout the segment, a somber background music plays.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens in a sunlit factory courtyard where women, some in colorful hijabs and casual clothing, are seen eating and socializing, with a scooter and yellow door in the background, as a narrator discusses the importance of factory suppliers respecting basic social and environmental rules. The scene then shifts to a low-angle view of a rough, debris-strewn road with a motorcycle in the foreground and pedestrians in the distance, while the narration questions if a particular issue is on 'Ortega's agenda today.' Subsequently, a black Mercedes-Benz sedan with its headlights on pulls into a dimly lit underground parking garage and stops. A man in a dark suit, identified by the narrator as Ortega, exits the vehicle and walks away, as the narrator reveals it's his 80th birthday and a huge surprise awaits him. The setting dramatically transitions to a well-lit, modern interior featuring a polished floor and a striking ceiling installation of reflective, bubble-like orbs. A diverse group of people stand clapping enthusiastically as Mr. Ortega and a woman in a long black coat emerge from an elevator. An announcer's voice exclaims, "Ladies and gentlemen, please welcome Mr. Ortega!" The man and woman share a warm, emotional embrace amidst continued applause, with the man placing his hand on her back and the woman resting her head on his shoulder, while the narrator reflects on Ortega's journey from a "little poor kid from Coruña" to the "emperor of the fashion retailer world."
